topic: New §4.2.7 — Eclipse geometry trilemma; dome's published Sun/Moon altitudes force a 1.3-km totality circle vs. observed 294-km path
trigger_note: EXP-254 is queued as target_type=section-new (proposal review before integration). The target content (replacement_html and SVG files) lives in the EXP file; sections.json has not yet been modified.
recommendation_summary: Section is structurally sound, math reproduces from scratch, cross-references all resolve. Approve for integration AFTER three moderate edits and pre-emption of one defensive opening the dome is likely to use.
our_argument_summary: EXP-254 introduces a new §4.2.7 that runs the August 2026 eclipse through the dome's own published parameters (h_Sun=5,733 km, h_Moon=2,534 km) using NASA Besselian-element angular sizes. The dome's geometry produces a totality circle ~1.3 km in diameter with single-observer duration ~0.9 sec, vs the observed 294-km path with up to 138-sec totality. The section then frames a trilemma — the dome must concede the parameters are wrong (option 1), borrow globe-geometry station selection (option 2), or invoke a non-occultation aetheric mechanism (option 3) — and shows the dome's current published predictions implicitly choose option 2 under option 1's parameters, an internally inconsistent combination. Two new SVG illustrations support the prose. The section is positioned as a parallel commitment to §4.2.6's magnetic-window revisit clause.

math_reproduction.method: Independent Python re-derivation from first principles using the section's stated inputs

results.L_u umbra cone length (km): 2681.1 — section claims 2,681 ✓ (disc IS inside cone, margin 147 km)

similarity_scaling_to_recover_observed.comparison_to_standard: Standard moon at 384,400 km — required dome moon (566k) is 1.47× standard moon (in the right neighborhood). Standard sun at 150M km — required dome sun (1.28M) is still 117× shorter than standard sun. So 'comparable to the standard model' is fair for the moon, loose for the sun. The section's wording is defensible but could be tightened (see hole #2).

math_reproduction.verdict: All math reproduces. The section's numbers are not just internally consistent; they're correct from first principles. Operator/analyst's correction of the original draft formula (operator used a wrong umbra-radius formula yielding 22 km diameter; analyst corrected to 1.3 km) is itself correct — confirmed by independent derivation.

cross_reference_check.p4-422 (4.2.2 misstatement-of-globe-pred: EXISTS in sections.json — confirmed via grep. EXP-253 (queue_id 264) integrated this section earlier today. Forward-reference resolves.
cross_reference_check.p4-424 (4.2.4 FSF=tan(latitude) tautolog: EXISTS. Confirmed §4.2.4 contains the FSF tan(latitude) identity exactly as the new §4.2.7 cites it.
cross_reference_check.p4-426 (4.2.6 magnetic-window revisit cl: EXISTS. Confirmed §4.2.6 contains the August 2026 magnetic-window revisit clause exactly as the new §4.2.7 cites it (parallel commitment framing).
cross_reference_check.Insertion target anchors p4-43-2 and p4-: BOTH EXIST. Insertion-anchor substring `
cross_reference_check.verdict: All cross-references resolve. Integration sequencing is safe — EXP-253 has already integrated 4.2.2, so the forward-reference to #p4-422 will land on the correct (newly rewritten) content.

dome_content_check.method: Read raw-text/03-predictions.txt and raw-text/05-model.txt for dome's actual eclipse content
findings: Dome's eclipse formula is `delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF`. The section addresses FSF parasitism via 4.2.4 cross-reference. The section's 4.2.7 argument extends this by addressing where 'coverage' values come from — see 'stronger argument' below.

Dome's eclipse mechanism is described as 'electromagnetic-aetheric in nature ... The aetheric field is perturbed by the geometric shadow blocking the field interaction between sun and dome. The effect follows the shadow, not the clock.' This is a HYBRID claim — aetheric mediation but geometric occultation as the source. Section's treatment as occultation-based is correct.

CRITICAL DEFENDER OPENING the section does not pre-empt: dome publishes TWO sun altitudes — 'Sun altitude (Observation): 5,733 km (optical illusion due to aetheric refraction)' and 'Sun altitude (Physical): < 4,200 km (firmament limit)'. A defender can argue the geometric calculation should use PHYSICAL altitudes, not OBSERVED altitudes, and that aetheric refraction warps the actual shadow. See hole #1 below.

options_considered: Option 2 (Borrowed — globe geometry imported): covered, and shown to be the dome's implicit current choice.
Option 3 (Aetheric — no occultation): covered, with adequate counter-argument that station-localization requires geometric path.
additional_options_probed: Refractive geometry: dome's observed/physical altitude distinction implies a refractive correction. This is a fourth defensive position. It collapses into option 1 (parameters wrong — use physical altitudes) OR option 3 (aetheric mechanism — refraction is an aetheric effect). When I redo the math using physical altitudes (h_Sun=4200, h_Moon~1860 by similar scaling, with proportionally smaller physical radii), totality diameter drops to ~0.9 km — STILL tiny. Argument is robust under either altitude convention. But the section currently does NOT make this robustness explicit; a dome defender will exploit the gap.
Refusal to engage: addressed in anticipated_objections #4 (refusal IS option 2 in disguise — accepting the inconsistency).
Higher-dimensional / non-Euclidean dome geometry: not published anywhere in dome material; if invoked it falls under option 3.
Verdict: trilemma is structurally exhaustive at the published-content level, but the refractive defensive opening should be pre-empted (see hole #1).

holes_found.description: Section does not pre-empt the 'observed vs physical altitude' defensive opening. The dome publishes both 'Sun altitude (Observation): 5,733 km (optical illusion due to aetheric refraction)' and 'Sun altitude (Physical): < 4,200 km (firmament limit)'. A defender can argue the section's geometric shadow calculation should use physical altitudes — or, more powerfully, that aetheric refraction makes simple geometric shadow calculation inapplicable. Without preemption, the section invites a 'you used the wrong numbers' rebuttal.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one paragraph or footnote in the §4.2.7 prose, ideally before the Counterargument section. Suggested wording: 'The dome publishes the Sun altitude as both an observed value (5,733 km, attributed to aetheric refraction) and a physical value (<4,200 km, firmament limit). The geometric calculation above uses observed altitudes because the visible eclipse follows light paths, not straight-line geometry from the unobserved physical Sun. Repeating the calculation with the dome's physical altitudes (h_Sun ≤ 4,200, h_Moon scaled proportionally) yields a totality circle smaller still — about 0.9 km diameter — confirming the geometric mismatch is robust to either altitude convention. A dome that wishes to recover the observed 294-km path via refraction must publish a refractive-shadow mechanism that is (a) not derivable from the standard occultation formula, AND (b) produces the same path width AND station-by-station coverage values as standard physics. No such mechanism is published.'

holes_found.description: The phrasing 'The dome geometry does not produce a path; it produces a stationary spot under the Moon's transit' (in the prose) and 'No path — a stationary spot' (in the Figure-1 SVG annotation panel) is technically misleading. The dome geometry DOES produce a path — a 1.3-km-wide track tracing the Moon's transit on the disc. The path is ~220× narrower than observed and the per-observer dwell time is ~150× shorter, but it is still a path. A dome defender will (correctly) point out that the section misrepresents the dome's prediction by claiming 'no path.' The substantive critique is unaffected, but the rhetoric is exploitable.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'The dome geometry does not produce a path; it produces a stationary spot under the Moon's transit' with: 'The dome geometry produces a narrow ~1.3-km-wide track tracing the Moon's transit overhead, vs. the observed 294-km swath — a ratio of roughly 1:220 in width.' In the SVG eclipse-geometry-comparison.svg, replace the panel annotation 'No path — a stationary spot.' with 'A 1.3-km-wide track — 1/220 of the observed path width.' Both edits preserve the argument and remove the rebuttal opening.

holes_found.description: Lateral velocity of 87 km/min is presented in the prose as the only figure ('transits at ≈ 87 km/min (a 24-hour circuit of a 20,015-km disc)') without acknowledging it is an upper bound that assumes the Moon orbits the disc rim. Most flat-earth dome models put the Moon's orbital path much closer to the disc center (e.g., over the tropics). At a more typical mid-disc orbital radius — say, the latitude band corresponding to a 64°N globe eclipse track — the velocity drops to ~25 km/min, lengthening single-observer totality from 0.9 sec to ~3.1 sec. The argument remains decisive (3.1 sec vs. observed 138 sec is still a 44× shortfall), but the math_verification section already acknowledges this charity (point 6) — it just doesn't propagate into the prose. A defender will (rightly) say the prose chose the worst-case velocity.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one sentence after '...transits at ≈ 87 km/min (a 24-hour circuit of a 20,015-km disc)' such as: '(This is an upper bound assuming a rim-radius orbit. A typical dome moon orbiting closer to the disc center moves slower — ~25 km/min at a radius corresponding to mid-northern latitudes — which lengthens single-observer totality to about 3 seconds. The argument is robust either way: 3 seconds vs. the observed 138 seconds is still a ~45× shortfall.)' This converts a defensive opening into a strength.

holes_found.description: Figure 2 (eclipse-coverage-vs-time-hartland.svg) labels the globe curve 'Globe physics — Chapman-Sq mechanism' twice. The y-axis is 'Sun obscuration' (visible light blockage), but the Chapman-Sq mechanism is the ionospheric-conductivity dynamo that produces magnetic anomalies — not the visible obscuration itself. Visible obscuration follows from straight-line eclipse geometry (NASA Besselian elements), not from Chapman-Sq dynamics. The mislabel will read as a category confusion to anyone familiar with eclipse physics.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'Globe physics (Chapman-Sq mechanism)' and 'Globe physics — Chapman-Sq mechanism' annotations in eclipse-coverage-vs-time-hartland.svg with 'Globe physics (NASA Besselian elements)' or simply 'Standard geometry'. The footer text 'Globe profile: NASA Besselian elements...' is already correct; the in-figure labels should match.

holes_found.description: The kernel-of-truth paragraph credits the dome's pre-registration practice ('OpenTimestamps anchored', 'pre-registered prediction') without acknowledging the project's standing finding (Timestamp Error tab) that the dome's OTS implementation timestamps observation files (status_history.json), not the prediction documents themselves. The section gives credit to a methodology the project has separately documented as broken on its key cryptographic move. A dome defender will catch this and use it to argue 'your own site is internally inconsistent — the kernel paragraph contradicts the Timestamp Error tab.'
holes_found.recommendation: In the kernel-of-truth paragraph, add a parenthetical: '(see the project's Timestamp Error tab for the separate question of whether the OpenTimestamps anchoring actually commits the predictions cryptographically)'. This preserves the methodological credit (predictions ARE made publicly in advance, which is more than most alternative cosmologies offer) while acknowledging the OTS-specific issue tracked elsewhere on the site. Two-line fix; preserves rhetorical strength.

holes_found.description: The section cites 'rows 23 and 28 of the 28-domain dashboard above' as the dome's solar-diameter and lunar-diameter domains. I could not find a 28-domain dashboard in the dome's raw text (raw-text/03-predictions.txt and 08-live-power.txt do not enumerate 28 domains by name). The reference may be to the dome's live.html dashboard which the project may not have a current snapshot of. If the row numbering is wrong or the dashboard structure has changed since the snapshot, the citation fails.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) verify against the current live.html / formula_runtime.json that solar-diameter and lunar-diameter domains exist with row numbers 23 and 28, OR (b) replace the specific row numbers with a generic citation: 'as documented in formula_runtime.json's solar-diameter and lunar-diameter domains'. Option (b) is safer; row numbering on a live-updating dashboard is fragile.

holes_found.description: Hartland's 88% peak obscuration figure (in Figure 2 and prose) is asserted without an explicit per-station citation. NASA's published station-by-station data (e.g., timeanddate.com or eclipsewise.com) should give a Hartland-specific magnitude/obscuration value. If the 88% figure is off by more than ~2-3 percentage points from the canonical published value, a defender could exploit it.
holes_found.recommendation: Cross-check 88% against timeanddate.com/eclipse for Hartland (51.0°N, 4.48°W) on 2026-08-12 OR cite a specific computed value from NASA's path-and-magnitude tables (the URL the section cites covers path width and duration but not station-by-station coverage). If 88% is correct, add a footnote with the source. If different, update.

stronger_arguments.description: Both inputs to the dome's eclipse formula are parasitic. The dome's published eclipse formula is `delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF`. §4.2.4 already establishes that FSF reduces to tan(latitude)/tan(ref_lat) — a globe-geographic-coordinate identity that contributes nothing dome-specific. §4.2.7 establishes (as a side consequence of the trilemma) that the per-station 'coverage' values (E001 Ebro 0.95, E005 Hartland 0.80, E007 Canary 0.72, etc.) cannot be derived from the dome's geometry — which yields a binary 0% or ~100% based on whether a station falls within the 1.3-km-wide track. Smooth real-valued coverage like 0.80 must come from globe physics. So BOTH multiplicative inputs to the dome's eclipse prediction are globe-derived; only the multiplicative 'baseline' (a fitted constant from BOU 2017) is dome-original. This is a stronger close than the trilemma alone — it shows the dome's eclipse prediction is essentially a globe-physics calculation with a dome-fitted constant in front.
sources: raw-text/03-predictions.txt: 'Formula: delta_Z = baseline x coverage x FSF'
stronger_arguments.recommendation: Optional: add one closing sentence before or after the Counterargument paragraph: 'Combined with §4.2.4, this exhausts both multiplicative inputs to the dome's eclipse formula δZ = baseline × coverage × FSF: FSF reduces to a globe-latitude identity, and coverage cannot be derived from dome geometry. Only the scalar baseline (a fitted constant from the 2017 BOU eclipse) remains dome-original.' This is a strong rhetorical close.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome geometry is barely 'total' for THIS eclipse. Cone length 2,681 km vs disc altitude 2,534 km gives a margin of only 147 km. For an eclipse with magnitude only slightly above 1.0 (i.e., Moon's apparent angular size barely exceeding Sun's), the cone would not reach the disc and the dome would predict NO totality at all — only annularity. NASA's published list of upcoming total eclipses with magnitudes 1.00–1.02 includes several where the dome geometry would produce zero totality. A consistency check across multiple eclipses would expose the dome's accidental dependence on this eclipse's specific magnitude.
sources: NASA SE catalog magnitudes for 2026-2030 total eclipses
stronger_arguments.recommendation: Optional, only if section length permits — could be a footnote: 'The umbra-cone length (2,681 km) exceeds the moon altitude (2,534 km) by only 147 km. For eclipses with magnitude barely above 1.0, the dome cone would not reach the disc surface — the dome would predict NO totality, only annularity, even when standard physics predicts a clear total eclipse. The Aug 12 2026 magnitude of 1.0386 just barely places the dome in the totality regime; this dependence on a specific eclipse's magnitude is itself non-physical.' Optional — adds robustness but lengthens the section.

kernel_of_truth.description: The section's own kernel-of-truth treatment is well-handled: it credits pre-registration practice (the methodological act of writing predictions down in advance) without crediting the predictions themselves. The dome's eclipse page IS more concrete and pre-registered than virtually any alternative-cosmology site — named stations, specific magnitudes, error bars, public commitment in advance.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Pre-registration is necessary but not sufficient for scientific credit. The dome publicly committed to predictions, which earns methodological points; but the predictions themselves rest on a geometric inconsistency the kernel does not resolve. Methodology gets credit; geometry does not. The kernel paragraph in the EXP makes this distinction clearly. Hole H5 (above) notes one minor improvement — flagging the OTS-specific question to the project's Timestamp Error tab.

eclipse-geometry-comparison.svg.geometric_faithfulness: Right panel altitudes are vertically to scale (15.0 km/px confirmed). Horizontal annotations (1.3-km totality, 85-km penumbra) are explicitly noted as not-to-scale and are correctly labeled. Left panel is schematic with break-line markers indicating distance — appropriate for a comparison.
eclipse-geometry-comparison.svg.annotation_correctness: Numbers in the right-panel info box (1.3 km totality, 0.9 sec at one observer, ratios 1/220 and 1/150) all verify against my independent math.
eclipse-geometry-comparison.svg.issue: Annotation 'No path — a stationary spot.' is technically misleading (see hole H2). Recommend rewording.
eclipse-geometry-comparison.svg.alt_text_completeness: Title and desc are present, descriptive, and accurate. Comprehensive enough for screen-reader users.
eclipse-geometry-comparison.svg.DIRECTIVE-20260422-003_compliance: Uses CSS variables (var(--heading), var(--ink), var(--semantic-warn), etc.) for theming consistency. Typography uses Inter and JetBrains Mono per project conventions. No competing visual elements; clear two-panel split. Compliant.

eclipse-coverage-vs-time-hartland.svg.geometric_faithfulness: Time axis 09:00-13:00 UTC. Globe curve approximated as smooth bell from 09:55 to 12:42 peaking at 11:18 (~88%). Dome curve flat-zero with sub-second spike at 11:18. Both are conceptually correct.
eclipse-coverage-vs-time-hartland.svg.issue: 'Globe physics (Chapman-Sq mechanism)' label is wrong — Chapman-Sq is the magnetic-anomaly mechanism, not the visible-obscuration mechanism. See hole H4.
eclipse-coverage-vs-time-hartland.svg.alt_text_completeness: Comprehensive. Title and desc explain both curves clearly.
eclipse-coverage-vs-time-hartland.svg.DIRECTIVE-20260422-003_compliance: CSS-variable theming, consistent typography, clear axis labels and gridlines. Compliant once H4 is fixed.

svg_audit.verdict: Both SVGs are well-constructed and accessible. Two minor annotation fixes needed (H2 and H4). No structural rework required.

acceptance_criteria_review.<img> tags resolve: Paths ./assets/eclipse-geometry-comparison.svg and ./assets/eclipse-coverage-vs-time-hartland.svg are correct relative paths from docs/.
acceptance_criteria_review.Trilemma is exactly three options: Three options. Hole H1 recommends pre-empting a fourth defensive opening (refraction) without expanding the trilemma itself.

internal_consistency_check.with §4.2.2 (misstatement of globe predi: Forward-reference handled correctly — §4.2.7's first paragraph explicitly says 'This argument is independent of, and complementary to, the misstatement-of-globe-prediction problem we establish at the top of 4.2.2.' Per-AMENDMENT-001, the misstatement framing was deliberately separated into 4.2.2 (delivered as EXP-253). No overlap conflict.
internal_consistency_check.with §4.2.4 (FSF tautology): Cross-reference is faithful. §4.2.4 establishes FSF=tan(latitude); §4.2.7's option-2 (borrowed station selection) cites this to argue station selection is independently parasitic. Compatible.
internal_consistency_check.with §4.2.6 (magnetic-window revisit): Parallel-commitment framing is appropriate. §4.2.6 commits to revisit on magnetic data; §4.2.7 commits to revisit on a coherent V51-internal eclipse mechanism. Same standard, different test. Compatible.
internal_consistency_check.with project's Timestamp Error tab: Minor tension — kernel-of-truth credits pre-registration without acknowledging the OTS-specific issue tracked on the Timestamp Error tab. See hole H5 for fix.
internal_consistency_check.verdict: No internal contradictions. One minor tension flagged.

proposal_package_review.verdict_flip_plausibility: Section's verdict is 'concession required' rather than a hard refute. The trilemma framing leaves room for the dome to publish a coherent V51-internal mechanism (option 3 done right) that would change the section's verdict. The revisit clause makes this commitment explicit. Plausible verdict-flip path is well-defined.
proposal_package_review.alternatives_considered_in_EXP: EXP file's anticipated_objections handles five potential pushbacks adequately (aetheric placeholder, lateral velocity charity, kernel-of-truth concern, refusal-to-choose, angular-size precision). Hole H1 identifies one additional defensive opening (observed/physical altitude refraction) that is NOT covered.
proposal_package_review.unknowns_acknowledged: EXP acknowledges: (a) lateral velocity is upper bound (math_verification point 6 — but doesn't propagate to prose, hole H3), (b) absence of aetheric eclipse mechanism is conditional on dome's current published material (correctly states 'until then, occultation is the only mechanism on the table'), (c) ratio claims are explicitly cross-checked.
proposal_package_review.new_tag_naming: N/A — this is a section addition, not a new code_analysis tag.
proposal_package_review.verdict: Proposal is rigorous. Six minor-to-moderate revisions recommended (H1-H6); none block integration. H1 is the most important — preempts the most likely defender pushback.

recommendation_to_decider.integration_decision: APPROVE WITH FIXES — Apply H1, H2, H3 (moderate edits to prose) and H4, H5, H6 (minor SVG/footnote edits) before integrating. Holes H7 (Hartland 88% citation) is non-blocking — can be deferred to a later patch if NASA station data lookup is non-trivial. Stronger arguments S1 (eclipse formula inputs both parasitic) and S2 (cone barely reaches disc — magnitude dependence) are optional polish — recommended for S1, defer S2 unless section length permits.
recommendation_to_decider.suggested_sequencing: 1) Apply H1 prose addition (refraction preemption). 2) Apply H2 prose+SVG edits (replace 'stationary spot' with 'narrow track'). 3) Apply H3 prose footnote (lateral velocity charity). 4) Apply H4 SVG label fix (Chapman-Sq → NASA Besselian). 5) Apply H5 prose parenthetical (Timestamp Error tab cross-link). 6) Apply H6 prose generalization (drop specific row numbers). 7) Integrate via decider_instructions substring match. 8) Run build + tests. 9) Re-queue for curmudgeon spot-check after integration.
recommendation_to_decider.severity_classification_for_batching: moderate — three moderate holes mean this is NOT a batchable singleton minor patch; the post-integration re-review will need full singleton attention.

deeper_analysis: EXP-254 is the strongest piece of analysis we've produced for the §4.2 cluster since the FSF tautology in 4.2.4. The math reproduces from first principles, the trilemma structure is exhaustive at the published-content level, and the integration with §4.2.6's parallel commitment is rhetorically strong. The section converts what could have been a vague 'this geometry doesn't work' complaint into a concrete forced-choice with named consequences and a public revisit clause. Six fixable issues — none structural — are flagged. The most important is H1 (refraction preemption), because the dome's own published distinction between observed and physical sun altitude is exactly the kind of detail a sophisticated defender will use to dismiss the section's geometric calculation. Pre-empting this turns a possible attack into a strength. The 'stationary spot' framing in H2 is a pure rhetoric error that hands a defender a free 'they misrepresent us' move; the substantive critique is unaffected by the wording. The S1 stronger argument — that BOTH multiplicative inputs to the dome's eclipse formula are parasitic — is a genuinely strong rhetorical close that follows directly from connecting 4.2.4 and 4.2.7. Applying that closure would tie the §4.2 cluster together rhetorically. Recommended action: minor_edit (apply H1-H6 + S1), then integrate.
summary_for_decider: Approve EXP-254 for integration after applying six edits. Three moderate (H1: pre-empt refractive-altitude defender opening; H2: replace 'stationary spot' framing in prose+SVG; H3: footnote lateral-velocity charity), three minor (H4: fix Chapman-Sq mislabel in Figure 2; H5: cross-link Timestamp Error tab in kernel; H6: drop hard-coded dashboard row numbers). One stronger argument (S1: both formula inputs parasitic) is recommended for inclusion as a closing sentence. None block integration. After integration, queue for a singleton spot-check (not batchable).
consideration_of_other_side: A defender's strongest available pushbacks against this section, in order of force: (1) 'You used observed altitudes, but our model says those are an aetheric-refraction artifact — use physical altitudes' — addressed by H1. (2) 'You picked the worst-case lateral velocity (rim orbit) when our moon orbits much closer to disc center' — addressed by H3. (3) 'The dome doesn't claim an inert geometric occultation; the mechanism is aetheric, so geometric shadow calculations don't apply' — addressed in the section's existing Counterargument paragraph and in H1's refractive footnote. (4) 'You said no path; we predict a path, just narrower' — addressed by H2. The section's structural argument survives ALL of these pushbacks (the math is robust under physical altitudes, slow velocity, aetheric mediation, and accurate path-language), but the prose currently doesn't make that survival visible. The recommended edits exist solely to convert latent strength into visible strength. Without these edits, a smart defender can score easy rhetorical points without touching the load-bearing math.
